{{short description|Sociological concept}}
'''Myth of meritocracy''' is a phrase arguing that [[meritocracy]], or achieving upward [[social mobility]] through one's own merits regardless of one's [[social position]], is not widely attainable in [[Capitalism|capitalist]] societies because of inherent contradictions. Meritocracy is argued to be a [[myth]] because, despite being promoted as an open and accessible method of achieving upward class mobility under [[Neoliberalism|neoliberal]] or [[free market]] capitalism, wealth disparity and limited class mobility remain widespread, regardless of individual [[work ethic]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Castilla |first1=Emilio J. |last2=Benard |first2=Stephen |title=The Paradox of Meritocracy in Organizations |journal=[[Administrative Science Quarterly]] |date=2010 |volume=55 |issue=4 |pages=543–676 |doi=10.2189/asqu.2010.55.4.543 |hdl=1721.1/65884 |hdl-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Morgan |first1=John |last2=Tumlinson |first2=Justin |last3=Várdy |first3=Felix |title=The limits of meritocracy |journal=[[Journal of Economic Theory]] |date=2022 |volume=201 |article-number=105414 |doi=10.1016/j.jet.2022.105414|hdl=10871/129789 |hdl-access=free }}</ref><ref name=":5">{{Cite book |last1=Cooper |first1=Jewell E. |title=Developing Critical Cultural Competence: A Guide for 21st-Century Educators |last2=He |first2=Ye |last3=Levin |first3=Barbara B. |publisher=[[SAGE Publications]] |year=2011 |isbn=978-1-4522-6927-6 |pages=14–15}}</ref><ref>{{cite press release |last1=Grover |first1=Ed |title=The myth of meritocracy is increasing inequality, book argues |url=https://phys.org/news/2017-10-myth-meritocracy-inequality.html |work=Phys.org |publisher=City University London |date=25 October 2017 }}</ref> Some scholars argue that the wealth disparity has even increased because the "myth" of meritocracy has been so effectively promoted and defended by the [[Politics|political]] and private [[elite]] through [[Mass media|the media]], [[education]], [[Corporate Culture|corporate culture]], and elsewhere.{{sfn|Littler|2017|p=139}}<ref>{{Cite web |last=Markovits |first=Daniel |date=9 July 2019 |title=How the 'meritocracy' feeds inequality |url=https://www.cnn.com/videos/tv/2019/09/07/how-the-meritocracy-feeds-inequality.cnn |website=[[CNN]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Cooper |first=Marianne |date=1 December 2015 |title=The False Promise of Meritocracy |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/business/archive/2015/12/meritocracy/418074/ |website=[[The Atlantic]]}}</ref> 

== History ==
Issues with meritocracy are not new. The word was coined and popularized as a [[pejorative]] but its usage has meliorated.  The first known use of the term was by sociologist [[Alan Fox (sociologist)|Alan Fox]] in the journal ''Socialist Commentary'' in 1956.{{sfn|Littler|2017|p=32}} It was then popularized by sociologist [[Michael Dunlop Young]], who used the term in his dystopian political and satirical book ''[[The Rise of the Meritocracy]]'' in 1958.<ref name="Fox">{{cite news |last=Fox |first=Margalit |date=25 January 2002 |title=Michael Young, 86, Scholar; Coined, Mocked 'Meritocracy' |work=The New York Times |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2002/01/25/world/michael-young-86-scholar-coined-mocked-meritocracy.html |access-date=19 February 2017 |archive-date=7 November 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161107053854/http://www.nytimes.com/2002/01/25/world/michael-young-86-scholar-coined-mocked-meritocracy.html |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Mijs |first1=Jonathan J.B. |last2=Savage |first2=Mike |title=Meritocracy, Elitism and Inequality |journal=[[The Political Quarterly]] |date=2020 |volume=91 |issue=2 |pages=397–404 |doi=10.1111/1467-923X.12828 |doi-access=free|hdl=1765/135370 |hdl-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Sachs-Cobbe |first1=Benjamin |title=Recent Work on Meritocracy |journal=[[Analysis (journal)|Analysis]] |date=2023 |volume=83 |issue=1 |pages=171–185 |doi=10.1093/analys/anac091 |doi-access=free|hdl=10023/28168 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>  The word was adopted into the English language without the negative connotations that Young intended it to have and was embraced by supporters of the philosophy. Young expressed his disappointment in the embrace of this word and philosophy by the [[Labour Party (UK)|Labour Party]] under [[Tony Blair]] in ''[[The Guardian]]'' in an article in 2001:
<blockquote>It is good sense to appoint individual people to jobs on their merit. It is the opposite when those who are judged to have merit of a particular kind harden into a new social class without room in it for others.<ref>{{cite news|last=Young|first=Michael|author-link=Michael Young, Baron Young of Dartington | title = Down with meritocracy | work = [[The Guardian]] | url = https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2001/jun/29/comment | date = 28 June 2001 | access-date = 23 August 2025|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20010703104851/http://www.guardian.co.uk:80/comment/story/0,3604,514207,00.html|archive-date=3 July 2001}}</ref></blockquote>

== Inevitability ==
In 1990, [[Michael Kinsley]] stated, "Inequalities of income, wealth, status are inevitable, and in a capitalist system even necessary."<ref name=":0">{{Cite news |last=Kinsley |first=Michael |date=18 January 1990 |title=The Myth of Meritocracy |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/opinions/1990/01/18/the-myth-of-meritocracy/ff68b614-f5bd-44e3-9c66-f1f0957a3a49/ |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]}}</ref> Rising wealth disparity increasingly undermines faith in the existence of meritocracy, as beliefs in [[equal opportunity]] and [[social equality]] lose credibility among lower classes who recognize the preexisting reality of limited class mobility as a feature of the neoliberal version of capitalism.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Thrasher |first=Steven W. |date=5 December 2015 |title=Income inequality happens by design. We can't fix it by tweaking capitalism |url=https://www.theguardian.com/commentisfree/2015/dec/05/income-inequality-policy-capitalism |website=[[The Guardian]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hasanov |first1=Fuad |last2=Izraeli |first2=Oded |date=February 2012 |title=How Much Inequality Is Necessary for Growth? |url=https://hbr.org/2012/01/how-much-inequality-is-necessary-for-growth |journal=[[Harvard Business Review]]}}</ref> At the same time, the elite use their comparatively greater wealth, power, and influence to unequally benefit themselves and ensure their continued [[upper class]] status at the expense of lower classes, which further undermines beliefs in the existence of meritocracy.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Levitz |first=Eric |date=20 September 2019 |title=Harvard's Affirmative Action for Rich Whites Exposes Myth of Meritocracy |url=https://nymag.com/intelligencer/2019/09/harvard-affirmative-action-white-people-meritocracy.html |website=[[New York Mag]]}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Francis |first=Lizzy |date=20 September 2019 |title=The Myth of Meritocracy Is The Real Criminal In The College Admissions Scandal |url=https://www.fatherly.com/love-money/myth-of-meritocracy-middle-class-families/ |website=[[Fatherly]]}}</ref>

[[Cornell University]] economist [[Robert H. Frank]] rejects meritocracy in his book ''[[Success and Luck: Good Fortune and the Myth of Meritocracy]]''.<ref>[[Princeton University Press]], 2016</ref> He describes how chance plays a significant role in deciding who gets what that is not objectively based on merit. He does not discount the importance of talent and hard work, but, using psychological studies, mathematical formulae, and examples, demonstrates that among groups of people performing at a high level, chance (luck) plays an enormous role in an individual's success.

== Function ==
The myth of meritocracy has been identified by scholars as a tool of the elite of a society to uphold and justify the reproduction of existing economic, social, and political hierarchies.<ref name=":5" />{{sfn|Littler|2017|p=139}}<ref name=":2">{{Cite magazine|title=The Caste of Merit: Engineering Education in India|url=https://www.foreignaffairs.com/reviews/capsule-review/2020-04-14/caste-merit-engineering-education-india|last=Nathan|first=Andrew J.|date=May 2020|website=Foreign Affairs}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book |last=Delay |first=Tad |title=Against: What Does the White Evangelical Want? |publisher=[[Cascade Books]] |year=2019 |isbn=978-1-5326-6846-3 |pages=140}}</ref>

=== Class mobility ===
The myth of meritocracy is used to maintain the belief that class mobility is widely attainable. As [[Daniel Markovits]] describes, "meritocracy excludes people outside of the elite, excludes middle class people and working class people from schooling, from good jobs, and from status and income, and then insults them by saying that the reason they're excluded is that they don't measure up, rather than that there's a structural block to their inclusion."<ref name=":1" /> Furthermore, Markovits explicitly denounces the myth of the purported "American meritocracy", which for him "has become precisely what it was invented to combat: a mechanism for the dynastic transmission of wealth and privilege across generations."<ref>Cited in: {{Cite journal|url=https://www.theguardian.com/news/2018/oct/19/the-myth-of-meritocracy-who-really-gets-what-they-deserve|title=The myth of meritocracy: who really gets what they deserve?|last=Appiah|first=Kwame Anthony|date=19 October 2018|journal=The Guardian|access-date=22 July 2021}}</ref> Phrases such as "pull yourself up by your bootstraps" have been identified as concealing the myth of meritocracy by placing the onus of upward class mobility solely on the individual while intentionally ignoring structural conditions.<ref name=":3" /> The minority of individuals who manage to overcome structural conditions and achieve upward class mobility are used as examples to support the idea that meritocracy exists.<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal |last1=Kraus |first1=Michael W. |last2=Tan |first2=Jacinth J.X. |date=May 2015 |title=Americans overestimate social class mobility |journal=[[Journal of Experimental Social Psychology]] |volume=58 |pages=101–111 |doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2015.01.005 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

In the [[United States]], people of lower classes are conditioned to believe in meritocracy, despite class mobility in the country being among the lowest in [[industrialized economies]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Gould |first=Elise |date=10 October 2012 |title=U.S. lags behind peer countries in mobility |url=https://www.epi.org/publication/usa-lags-peer-countries-mobility/ |website=[[Economic Policy Institute]]}}</ref><ref name=":3" /> In the U.S., 50% of a father's income position is inherited by his son. In contrast, the amount in Norway and Canada is less than 20%. Moreover, in the U.S. 8% of children raised in the bottom 20% of the income distribution are able to climb to the top 20% as adults, while the figure in Denmark is nearly double at 15%.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=The American Dream of upward mobility is broken. Look at the numbers|last1=Rank|first1=Mark R|last2=Eppard|first2=Lawrence M|journal=The Guardian|date=13 March 2021 |url=https://www.theguardian.com/commentisfree/2021/mar/13/american-dream-broken-upward-mobility-us?CMP=GTUS_email}}</ref> According to an academic study on why Americans overestimate class mobility, "research indicates that errors in social perception are driven by both informational factors—such as the lack of awareness of statistical information relevant to actual mobility trends—and motivational factors—the desire to believe that society is meritocratic."<ref name=":4" /> Americans are more inclined to believe in meritocracy out of the prospect that they will one day join the elite or upper class. Scholars have paralleled this belief to [[John Steinbeck]]'s notable quote that "the poor see themselves not as an exploited [[proletariat]] but as temporarily embarrassed millionaires."<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Alesina |first1=Alberto |last2=Stantcheva |first2=Stefanie |last3=Teso |first3=Edoardo |date=2018 |title=Intergenerational Mobility and Preferences for Redistribution |journal=[[American Economic Review]] |volume=108 |issue=2 |pages=521–554 |doi=10.1257/aer.20162015 |doi-access=free }}</ref> As academic Tad Delay states, "the fantasy of class mobility, of becoming ''[[Bourgeoisie|bourgeois]]'', is enough to defend the [[Aristocracy (class)|aristocracy]]."<ref name=":3" />

[[Robert Reich]] argues that many [[Americans]] still believe in meritocracy despite "the nation drifting ever-farther away from it."<ref>{{Cite web|title=Robert Reich: The myth of meritocracy|url=https://www.salon.com/2019/04/15/robert-reich-the-myth-of-meritocracy_partner/|last=Reich|first=Robert|date=15 April 2019|website=Salon}}</ref>

In India, the myth of meritocracy has been identified as a mechanism for the elite to justify the structure of the [[Caste system in India|caste system]].<ref name=":2" />

=== Racism ===
The myth of meritocracy has been identified by scholars as promoting the [[Color blindness (racial classification)|color blind]] philosophy that anyone, regardless of their [[Race (human categorization)|race]] or [[Ethnic group|ethnicity]], can succeed if they work hard enough. "This belief suggests that if a person of color is not succeeding at work (e.g. not getting promoted), it must be due to laziness or a lack of effort on that person's part," rather than a structural barrier, as described by [[Kevin Nadal]], Katie Griffen, and Yinglee Wong.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Nadal|first1=Kevin L.|title=Women as Transformational Leaders: From Grassroots to Global Interests|last2=Griffin|first2=Katie E.|last3=Wong|first3=Yinglee|publisher=ABC-CLIO|year=2011|isbn=978-0-313-38653-4|editor-last=Paludi|editor-first=Michele A.|pages=9–10|chapter=Gender, Racial, and Sexual Orientation Microaggressions|editor-last2=Coates|editor-first2=Breena E.}}</ref> The myth of meritocracy has been identified as a tool to both dismiss [[institutional racism]] and justify [[racism|racist attitudes]], while also serving as an argument against [[Affirmative action in the United States|affirmative action]] policies.<ref name=":5" /><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Halley |first1=Jean O'Malley |title=Seeing White: An Introduction to White Privilege and Race |last2=Eshleman |first2=Amy |last3=Mahadevan Vijaya |first3=Ramya |publisher=[[Rowman & Littlefield Publishers]] |year=2011 |isbn=978-1-4422-0307-5 |pages=191–192}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cock |first1=Jacklyn |url=https://archive.org/details/meltingpotsrainb0000cock |title=Melting Pots & Rainbow Nations: Conversations about Difference in the United States and South Africa |last2=Bernstein |first2=Alison R. |publisher=[[University of Illinois Press]] |year=2002 |isbn=978-0-252-07027-3 |pages=[https://archive.org/details/meltingpotsrainb0000cock/page/34 34] |url-access=registration}}</ref> The belief that the United States is a meritocracy is most accepted as an accurate reflection of reality among young, upper class whites and least accepted as an accurate reflection of reality among older, working class, people of color.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Reynolds |first1=Jeremy |last2=Xian |first2=He |title=Perceptions of meritocracy in the land of opportunity |journal=Research in Social Stratification and Mobility |date=June 2014 |volume=36 |pages=121–137 |doi=10.1016/j.rssm.2014.03.001 }}</ref>

=== Tyranny of merit ===
[[Harvard]] philosopher [[Michael Sandel]] in his latest book (2020) makes a case against meritocracy, calling it a "tyranny". Ongoing stalled social mobility and increasing inequality are laying bare the crass delusion of the [[American Dream]], and the promise "you can make it if you want and try". The latter, according to Sandel, is the main culprit of the anger and frustration which brought some Western countries towards [[populism]].<ref>[[Michael Sandel|Sandel, Michael]]. {{cite book |title=The Tyranny of Merit: What's Become of the Common Good? |publisher=[[Farrar, Straus and Giroux]] |year=2020 |isbn=978-0-374-28998-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/books/2020/sep/06/michael-sandel-the-populist-backlash-has-been-a-revolt-against-the-tyranny-of-merit|title=Michael Sandel: 'The populist backlash has been a revolt against the tyranny of merit'|last=Coman|first=Julian|journal=The Guardian|date=6 September 2020}}</ref>
